speaker: narrator
line: It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife.
ambience: soft fire crackling in a quiet English drawing room, faint birds chirping outside

speaker: narrator
line: However little known the feelings or views of such a man may be on his first entering a neighbourhood, this truth is so well fixed in the minds of the surrounding families, that he is considered the rightful property of some one or other of their daughters.
ambience: gentle wind brushing against windows, tea cups softly clinking

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘My dear Mr. Bennet,’ said his lady to him one day, ‘have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?’
ambience: tea cups rattling lightly on saucers near a warm fireplace

speaker: mr bennet
line: Mr. Bennet replied that he had not.
ambience: steady fire crackling in silence

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘But it is,’ returned she; ‘for Mrs. Long has just been here, and she told me all about it.’
ambience: quick footsteps and faint china clinking in the drawing room

speaker: narrator
line: Mr. Bennet made no answer.
ambience: quiet pause with fire softly crackling

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘Do you not want to know who has taken it?’ cried his wife impatiently.
ambience: chair creaking and fire crackling more prominently

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘YOU want to tell me, and I have no objection to hearing it.’
ambience: quiet room with faint wind outside the windows

speaker: narrator
line: This was invitation enough.
ambience: soft fire crackling and distant birds singing

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘Why, my dear, you must know, Mrs. Long says that Netherfield is taken by a young man of large fortune from the north of England; that he came down on Monday in a chaise and four to see the place, and was so much delighted with it, that he agreed with Mr. Morris immediately; that he is to take possession before Michaelmas, and some of his servants are to be in the house by the end of next week.’
ambience: animated movement around a cozy drawing room, tea cups clinking

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘What is his name?’
ambience: soft crackling fire and a quiet pause

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘Bingley.’
ambience: tea spoon softly tapping porcelain

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘Is he married or single?’
ambience: faint wind outside and fire crackling

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘Oh! Single, my dear, to be sure! A single man of large fortune; four or five thousand a year. What a fine thing for our girls!’
ambience: energetic chatter in a warm drawing room

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘How so? How can it affect them?’
ambience: quiet fire crackling with slight wind at the windows

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘My dear Mr. Bennet,’ replied his wife, ‘how can you be so tiresome! You must know that I am thinking of his marrying one of them.’
ambience: chair shifting sharply on wooden floorboards

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘Is that his design in settling here?’
ambience: fire crackling steadily

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘Design! Nonsense, how can you talk so! But it is very likely that he MAY fall in love with one of them, and therefore you must visit him as soon as he comes.’
ambience: tea cups rattling lightly with agitated movement

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘I see no occasion for that. You and the girls may go, or you may send them by themselves, which perhaps will be still better, for as you are as handsome as any of them, Mr. Bingley may like you the best of the party.’
ambience: fire crackling softly beneath the dialogue

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘My dear, you flatter me. I certainly HAVE had my share of beauty, but I do not pretend to be anything extraordinary now. When a woman has five grown-up daughters, she ought to give over thinking of her own beauty.’
ambience: soft laughter and tea cups gently clinking

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘In such cases, a woman has not often much beauty to think of.’
ambience: faint wind through the windows and crackling fire

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘But, my dear, you must indeed go and see Mr. Bingley when he comes into the neighbourhood.’
ambience: quiet room with low fire crackling

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘It is more than I engage for, I assure you.’
ambience: steady fire crackling

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘But consider your daughters. Only think what an establishment it would be for one of them. Sir William and Lady Lucas are determined to go, merely on that account, for in general, you know, they visit no newcomers. Indeed you must go, for it will be impossible for US to visit him if you do not.’
ambience: wind softly brushing the windows, tea cups faintly rattling

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘You are over-scrupulous, surely. I dare say Mr. Bingley will be very glad to see you; and I will send a few lines by you to assure him of my hearty consent to his marrying whichever he chooses of the girls; though I must throw in a good word for my little Lizzy.’
ambience: low crackling fire in a quiet room

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘I desire you will do no such thing. Lizzy is not a bit better than the others; and I am sure she is not half so handsome as Jane, nor half so good-humoured as Lydia. But you are always giving HER the preference.’
ambience: sharp movement of a chair and tea cup set down firmly

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘They have none of them much to recommend them,’ replied he; ‘they are all silly and ignorant like other girls; but Lizzy has something more of quickness than her sisters.’
ambience: fire crackling quietly in the background

speaker: mrs bennet
line: ‘Mr. Bennet, how CAN you abuse your own children in such a way? You take delight in vexing me. You have no compassion for my poor nerves.’
ambience: teacups rattling with agitation

speaker: mr bennet
line: ‘You mistake me, my dear. I have a high respect for your nerves. They are my old friends. I have heard you mention them with consideration these last twenty years at least.’
ambience: soft fire crackling in the drawing room

speaker: narrator
line: Mr. Bennet was so odd a mixture of quick parts, sarcastic humour, reserve, and caprice, that the experience of three-and-twenty years had been insufficient to make his wife understand his character.
ambience: quiet fire crackling and faint wind through windows

speaker: narrator
line: HER mind was less difficult to develop.
ambience: soft fire crackling in the background

speaker: narrator
line: She was a woman of mean understanding, little information, and uncertain temper.
ambience: gentle wind outside and low fire crackling

speaker: narrator
line: When she was discontented, she fancied herself nervous.
ambience: soft crackling fireplace and faint ticking clock

speaker: narrator
line: The business of her life was to get her daughters married; its solace was visiting and news.
ambience: quiet English drawing room with low fire crackling
